children.value:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
This subchapter will go into greater detail on how you can select/modify rows and columns in
First, let us load in the same dataset as the first subchapter in the
chapter. As a reminder, this dataset has beer sales across 50 continental states in the US. It is sourced from
Salience and Taxation: Theory and Evidence
by Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (AER 2009), and it includes 7 columns:
: the year the data was recorded
: the quantity of beer consumed, in thousands of gallons
: the ad valorem tax, as a percentage
: the excise tax, represented in dollars per case (24 cans) of beer
: the population of the state, in thousands

text/html.content:
st_name year c_beer beer_tax btax_dollars population salestax
0 AL 1970 33098 72.341130 2.370 3450 4.0
1 AL 1971 37598 69.304600 2.370 3497 4.0
2 AL 1972 42719 67.149190 2.370 3539 4.0
3 AL 1973 46203 63.217026 2.370 3580 4.0
4 AL 1974 49769 56.933796 2.370 3627 4.0
... ... ... ... ... ... ... ...
1703 WY 1999 12423 0.319894 0.045 492 4.0
1704 WY 2000 12595 0.309491 0.045 494 4.0
1705 WY 2001 12808 0.300928 0.045 494 4.0
1706 WY 2002 13191 0.296244 0.045 499 4.0
1707 WY 2003 15535 0.289643 0.045 501 4.0

1708 rows × 7 columns

children.value: Extracting Rows/Columns that Satisfy a Given Condition

children.label: Extracting Rows/Columns that Satisfy a Given Condition

children.value: An important task we want to accomplish when looking at data is to filter out all rows that satisfy a certain condition. In other words, we want to extract all the rows
a certain thing is accomplished. Conditional selection helps us achieve exactly this, extracting all rows that satisfy a certain condition.
, we briefly gave an example of how we can use
to extract all rows that match certain values. To be more precise, when we pass in certain values for
to use, they generate an array of True/False values and use that to determine which values to include. This is very similar to our prior discussion on
For example, let’s say we want all the data for the state of Alabama. Then, we would want all rows where
. We can generate a Series of True/False values corresponding to whether each row has a

text/plain.content:
0        True
1        True
2        True
3        True
4        True
        ...  
1703    False
1704    False
1705    False
1706    False
1707    False
Name: st_name, Length: 1708, dtype: bool

children.value: Now, to extract all rows where the state is Alabama, we can simply pass in this boolean series into the
df.loc[df['st_name'] == 'AL',:].head(5) # only showing the first 5 rows

text/html.content:
st_name year c_beer beer_tax btax_dollars population salestax
0 AL 1970 33098 72.341130 2.37 3450 4.0
1 AL 1971 37598 69.304600 2.37 3497 4.0
2 AL 1972 42719 67.149190 2.37 3539 4.0
3 AL 1973 46203 63.217026 2.37 3580 4.0
4 AL 1974 49769 56.933796 2.37 3627 4.0

children.value: Similarly, we can also use the context dependent
df[df['st_name'] == 'AL'].tail(5) # only showing the last 5 rows

text/html.content:
st_name year c_beer beer_tax btax_dollars population salestax
29 AL 1999 93828 16.847755 2.37 4430 4.0
30 AL 2000 94530 16.299862 2.37 4452 4.0
31 AL 2001 92893 15.848877 2.37 4466 4.0
32 AL 2002 94732 15.602203 2.37 4479 4.0
33 AL 2003 96188 15.254544 2.37 4501 4.0

children.value: We could also use the same technique to extract all columns that are integers (but not floats, so they do not contain decimals).

text/html.content:
year c_beer population
0 1970 33098 3450
1 1971 37598 3497
2 1972 42719 3539
3 1973 46203 3580
4 1974 49769 3627
... ... ... ...
1703 1999 12423 492
1704 2000 12595 494
1705 2001 12808 494
1706 2002 13191 499
1707 2003 15535 501

1708 rows × 3 columns

children.value: Alternatively, if we wanted to extract all the rows where the index is less than 30, we could similarly use the

text/plain.content: array([ True,  True,  True, ..., False, False, False])

children.value: df.iloc[df.index < 30,:].tail(5) # only showing the last 5 rows

children.value: As you can imagine, quickly and easily generating these boolean arrays/Series is very important for accessing our elements!
What if we have multiple conditions we want to filter on? Let’s say we want to look at all the rows where
is less than 0.5. We can generate boolean filters for both of those conditions.

text/plain.content:
0       False
1       False
2       False
3       False
4       False
        ...  
1703     True
1704     True
1705     True
1706     True
1707     True
Name: beer_tax, Length: 1708, dtype: bool

0       False
1       False
2       False
3       False
4       False
        ...  
1703     True
1704     True
1705     True
1706     True
1707     True
Name: btax_dollars, Length: 1708, dtype: bool

children.value: How can we combine both of these boolean arrays? Well, since we’re interested in cases where both of these are true, we can use the
operator is the logical way of expressing you only want the cases where elements from both the arrays are true.
The code is shown below. Note the parenthesis around both the boolean arrays - this is an absolutely crucial piece of syntax!

text/html.content:

79 AZ 1981 86283 2.345187 0.180 2810 4.0
80 AZ 1982 86249 2.209093 0.180 2890 4.0

operator sum to the number of columns with the
Finally, what if you’re interested in the cases where something isn’t true? For example, what if you’re interested in all the cases where the
? Well, then you can use the
operator, which logically represents the inverse of a boolean array (so it converts all true values into false and all false values intro true).

children.value: Here’s a table summarizing all the different logical operators to combine boolean arrays.
If you want to keep all rows where a certain column is in a list of given values, you can use the
method. This is logically equivalent to using the
operator multiple times, but it’s easier. For example, below we get all rows corresponding to the states of California, Florida, Texas and Nebraska.

text/plain.content:
1707    False
1706    False
1705    False
1704    False
1703    False
        ...  
4       False
3       False
2       False
1       False
0       False
Name: st_name, Length: 1708, dtype: bool

children.value: We can also use conditional selection to edit certain rows. For example, let’s say we found out we made an error with measuring
for the state of Alabama, and the true beer tax is actually 0.1 higher than what we’ve measured. We can easily use boolean filtering to select the columns where we’ve made this mistake and fix them!

text/html.content:
st_name year c_beer beer_tax btax_dollars population salestax
0 AL 1970 33098 72.441130 2.370 3450 4.0
1 AL 1971 37598 69.404600 2.370 3497 4.0
2 AL 1972 42719 67.249190 2.370 3539 4.0
3 AL 1973 46203 63.317026 2.370 3580 4.0
4 AL 1974 49769 57.033796 2.370 3627 4.0
... ... ... ... ... ... ... ...
1703 WY 1999 12423 0.319894 0.045 492 4.0
1704 WY 2000 12595 0.309491 0.045 494 4.0
1705 WY 2001 12808 0.300928 0.045 494 4.0
1706 WY 2002 13191 0.296244 0.045 499 4.0
1707 WY 2003 15535 0.289643 0.045 501 4.0

1708 rows × 7 columns

children.value: Now, we move on to modifying the columns in our DataFrames. The first task we will consider is how to add a new column.
Let’s say we want to add a new column where we calculate the total percentage taxed by combining the
. Both of these % taxes compound, so we will need to be careful with the math! Below, we calculate a series with the sum of these two taxes, as a percent.
Now, we want to set this series equal to a new column named
in our dataframe. The syntax for doing this follows

text/html.content:
st_name year c_beer beer_tax btax_dollars population salestax total_percent_tax
0 AL 1970 33098 72.341130 2.37 3450 4.0 79.234775
1 AL 1971 37598 69.304600 2.37 3497 4.0 76.076784
2 AL 1972 42719 67.149190 2.37 3539 4.0 73.835158
3 AL 1973 46203 63.217026 2.37 3580 4.0 69.745707
4 AL 1974 49769 56.933796 2.37 3627 4.0 63.211148

children.value: Voila, our new column has been added!
feels like a confusing name. Instead, let us change to
, a more informative name! To do so, we can use the DataFrame

children.value: method takes in a dictionary where all the keys are the old column names and all the values are the new column names.

methods just return a copy of the modified DataFrame for you to see. To change the original dataframe, you can either manually assign the new DataFrame to
df # original dataframe has not changed

prev.title: Loading in and Looking at the Data
